senses.g: oblique plural of ضَعِیف (za'īf, “poor; old person”)

ضلع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضلع (zil', “side, edge”), from Arabic ضِلْع (ḍilʕ, “side, edge, division”). Compare Nepali जिल्ला (jillā) and Marathi जिल्हा (jilhā).

ضم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضم (zamm), from Arabic ضَمّ (ḍamm).

ضمانت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضمانت (zamānat), from Arabic ضَمَانَة (ḍamāna) / ضَمان (ḍamān).

ضمیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضَمِیر (zamīr), from Arabic ضَمِير (ḍamīr). First attested in c. 1657 as Middle Hindi ضمیر (zamīr).

ضیافت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ضِیَافَت (ziyāfat), borrowed from Arabic ضِيَافَة (ḍiyāfa), from ضَافَ (ḍāfa).

senses.g: The twenty-second letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ض and followed by ظ.

طاعون.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طاعُون (tā'ūn), from Arabic طَاعُون (ṭāʕūn).

طاق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طاق (tâq), ultimately from Middle Persian [script needed] (tʾg /⁠tāg⁠/, “arch”).

senses.g: power; authority (ie. wordly powers) — army; military
power; authority (ie. wordly powers) — authority; government

طاقت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَاقَت (tāqat). First attested in c. 1599 as Middle Hindi طاقت (t̤aqt).

طاقتور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طاقتور (tāqat-war), the first element from Arabic طَاقَة (ṭāqa).

senses.g: Taliban (a Deobandi Islamist and largely Pashtun religious political movement founded in Afghanistan in 1994 by mullah Mohammad Omar, which has governed the country from 1996 to 2001 and since 2021)

طاؤس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَاوُوس (tāwūs), borrowed from Arabic طَاوُوس (ṭāwūs).

طبق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَبَق (tabaq), borrowed from Arabic طَبَق (ṭabaq), from طَابَق (ṭābaq), borrowed from Middle Persian tʾp̄k', from Proto-Iranian *tāpaka-.

طبقہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَبَقَه (tabaqa), borrowed from Arabic طَبَقَة (ṭabaqa).

طبی.e: Borrowed from Arabic طِبِّيّ (ṭibbiyy). By surface analysis, طِبّ (tibb, “medicine”) + ـی (-ī).

طبیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طبیب (tabib), from Arabic طَبِيب (ṭabīb).

senses.g: temperament, mind, disposition, mood, character, behavior

طبیعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَبِیعَت (tabī'at), borrowed from Arabic طَبِيعَة (ṭabīʕa).

senses.g: natural, innate, intrinsic, physical, physicist, pertaining to physics

طبیعی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَبِیعِی (tabī'ī), from Arabic طَبِيعِيّ (ṭabīʕiyy).

طرابلس.e: Borrowed from Arabic طَرَابُلُس (ṭarābulus), from Ancient Greek Τρίπολις (Trípolis).

ex.u: یہ کس طرح سے بنا ہے؟
ex.r: ye kis tarah se banā hai?
ex.e: By what method is this made?

senses.g: method, manner, mode — way (the manner or identified or described)

ex.u: یہ کس طرح کا سوال ہے؟
ex.r: ye kis tarah kā savāl hai?
ex.e: What type of question is this?

طرح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طرح (tarh, “plan, design”), from Arabic طَرْح (ṭarḥ, “teaching, deduction”).

طرز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَرْز (tarz), from Arabic طَرْز (ṭarz).

senses.g: oblique plural of طَرْز (tarz, “style; manner; mode”)
direct plural of طَرْز (tarz, “style; manner; mode”)

طرف دار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طرفدار (tarafdār). By surface analysis, طرف (taraf) + دار (-dār).

senses.g: a party-man, partisan; a follower; a sectary; an assistant

طرف دار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طرفدار (tarafdār). By surface analysis, طرف (taraf) + دار (-dār).

ex.u: نیا کام کرنے کا طریقہ اپنانا چاہیے۔
ex.r: nayā kām karne kā tarīqā apnānā cāhiye
ex.e: You should adopt a new method of working.

ex.u: اِس بات کرنے کا طریقہ صحیح نہیں ہے۔
ex.r: is bāt karne kā tarīqā sahīh nahī̃ hai.
ex.e: This manner of talking is not correct.

طریقہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَرِیقَه (tarīqa), from Arabic طَرِيقَة (ṭarīqa).

طشتری.e: Ultimately from Classical Persian تشت (tašt, “saucer, tray”) (whence طشت (tašt) / تشت (tašt))، from Middle Persian tšt' (tašt).

طعنہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَعْنَه (ta'na), borrowed from Arabic طَعْنَة (ṭaʕna), from طَعَنَ (ṭaʕana). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi طعنہ (t̤'nh /⁠ta'na⁠/).

طفل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طفل (tifl), from Arabic طِفْل (ṭifl).

senses.g: talaq, divorce (initiated by husband and not wife)

طلاق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طلاق (talāq), from Arabic طَلَاق (ṭalāq).

طلب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَلَب (talab), from Arabic طَلَب (ṭalab).

senses.g: vocative plural of طَلَب گَار (talab gār)
oblique plural of طَلَب گَار (talab gār)
alternative form of طَلَب گَار (talab gār)

طلعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طلعت (tal'at), from Arabic طَلْعَة (ṭalʕa).

senses.g: a male given name, Talat, from Arabic

طلعت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طلعت (tal'at), from Arabic طَلْعَة (ṭalʕa).

طلوع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طلوع (tulū'), from Arabic طُلُوع (ṭulūʕ).

طوائف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَوَائِف (tawā'if), ultimately borrowed from Arabic طَوائِف (ṭawāʔif).

senses.g: excellent, sweet, delicious and the like
name of a tree in Paradise, the fruit of which is said to be most delicious
a female given name, Tooba, from Arabic

طور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَور (tawr, “manner, style”), from Arabic طَوْر (ṭawr).

طوطا.e: Possibly from Classical Persian توته (tōta, “parrot”), طوطی (tūtī). Compare Punjabi ਤੋਤਾ (totā) / طوطا (t̤ot̤ā), Sindhi طوطو (t̤ot̤o) / तोतो (toto).

طوفان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طوفان (tūfān), from Arabic طُوفَان (ṭūfān).

senses.g: airplane; airship; plane (aircraft of any kind)

طیارہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طیاره (tayyāra), from Ottoman Turkish طیاره (tayyâre).

senses.g: a fragrant substance, a perfume, odour
the most excellent (of any sort or thing), the essence, the cream (of)
a flying thing, a winged creature, a bird, &c

طیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طَیْر (tayr), from Arabic طَيْر (ṭayr).

طے.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian طی (tay), from Arabic طي.

senses.g: The twenty-third letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ط and followed by ع.

ظاہر.e: From Classical Persian ظاهر (zāhir), from Arabic ظَاهِر (ẓāhir).

ظرف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ظَرْف (zarf), from Arabic ظَرْف (ẓarf).

ظروف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ظُرُوف (zurūf), from Arabic ظُرُوف (ẓurūf)

ظفر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ظفر (zafar), from Arabic ظَفَر (ẓafar).

ظلم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ظُلْم (zulm), borrowed from Arabic ظُلْم (ẓulm).

ظہرانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ظُهْرَانَه (zuhrāna). By surface analysis, ظُہْر (zuhr, “midday; noon; dhuhr”) + ـَانَہ (āna).

ظہور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ظُهُور (zuhūr). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi ظہور (z̤hvr).

senses.g: a male given name, Zuhoor or Zahoor, from Arabic

ظہور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ظُهُور (zuhūr). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi ظہور (z̤hvr).

senses.g: The twenty-fourth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ظ and followed by غ.

عابد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عابد ('ābid), itself from Arabic عَابِد (ʕābid).
Borrowed from Classical Persian عابد ('ābid), itself from Arabic عَابِد (ʕābid).
Borrowed from Classical Persian عابد ('ābid), itself from Arabic عَابِد (ʕābid).

senses.g: a female given name, Abida, from Arabic

عاجزی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَاجِزِی (ājizī), from Arabic عَاجِزٍ (ʕājizin). By surface analysis, عَاجِز ('ājiz, “humble”) + ی (ī)

عادت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عادت ('ādat), from Arabic عَادَة (ʕāda).

عادی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عادی ('ādī), from Arabic عَادِيّ (ʕādiyy).

senses.g: Arif Wala (a city in Pakpattan district, Punjab, Pakistan)

عاری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عاری ('ārī), from Arabic عَارِي (ʕārī).

عاشق.e: First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi عاشِق ('aśq), borrowed from Classical Persian عَاشِق (āšiq), from Arabic عَاشِق (ʕāšiq).

عاصی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عاصی ('āsī), from Arabic عَاصٍ (ʕāṣin).
Borrowed from Classical Persian عاصی ('āsī), from Arabic عَاصٍ (ʕāṣin).

عالم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَالَم (ālam), borrowed from Arabic عَالَم (ʕālam), borrowed from Aramaic עָלְמָא or Classical Syriac ܥܳܠܡܳܐ.
Borrowed from Classical Persian عَالِم (ālim), borrowed from Arabic عَالِم (ʕālim), from عَلِمَ (ʕalima).
Borrowed from Classical Persian عَالِم (ālim), borrowed from Arabic عَالِم (ʕālim), from عَلِمَ (ʕalima).

senses.g: high, elevated, exalted, eminent, noble, grand, sublime

عالیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَالِیَه (āliya), from Arabic عَالِيَة (ʕāliya).

senses.g: a female given name from Arabic

عالیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَالِیَه (āliya), from Arabic عَالِيَة (ʕāliya).

عام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَامّ (āmm), borrowed from Arabic عَامّ (ʕāmm).

عام طور پر.e: From عام ('ām, “common; general”) + طَور (taur, “manner”) + پَر (par, “postposition marker – in, on”).

عام طور سے.e: Combination of عامّ ('āmm, “common, general, ordinary”) + طَور (taur, “manner”) + postposition سے (se, “from, by”).

عاملہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عامله ('āmila), itself from Arabic عَامِلَة (ʕāmila), from the root ع م ل (ʕ m l).

عائلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَائِلَه (ā'ila), from Arabic عَائِلَة (ʕāʔila).

عبادت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِبَادَت (ibādat), borrowed from Arabic عِبَادَة (ʕibāda), from عَبَدَ (ʕabada).

عبادت گاہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِبَادَتْگَاه (ibādatgāh). By surface analysis, عِبادَت ('ibādat, “worship”) + گاہ (gāh, “place, centre”).

senses.g: A religious person, devotee, a worshipper, an adorer.
a male given name, Abdullah, from Arabic, meaning “slave of God, servant of God”

عبد اللہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic عَبْد اللَّٰه (ʕabd allāh).

عبرانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عبرانی ('ibrānī), from Arabic عِبْرِيّ (ʕibriyy), ultimately from Hebrew עִבְרִי ('ivrí, ʿiḇrī).
Borrowed from Classical Persian عبرانی ('ibrānī), from Arabic عِبْرِيّ (ʕibriyy), ultimately from Hebrew עִבְרִי ('ivrí, ʿiḇrī).

عبری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عبری ('ibrī), from Arabic عِبْرِيّ (ʕibriyy), from Hebrew עִבְרִי ('ivrí, ʿiḇrī).
Borrowed from Classical Persian عبری ('ibrī), from Arabic عِبْرِيّ (ʕibriyy), from Hebrew עִבְרִי ('ivrí, ʿiḇrī).

senses.g: Uthman (Uthman bin Affan, third Islamic caliph and a companion of Muhammad)
a male given name, Osman or Usman, from Arabic, equivalent to English Uthman

عثمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عُثْمَان (usmān), from Arabic عُثْمَان (ʕuṯmān).

عجائب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَجَائِب (ajā'ib), from Arabic عَجَائِب (ʕajāʔib), plural of عَجِيبَة (ʕajība).

عجائب گھر.e: From عَجائِب ('ajāib) + گَھر (ghar), literally “wonder-room (cabinet of curiosities)”.

عجب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَجَب (ajab), from Arabic عَجَب (ʕajab).

ex.e: the seven wonders of the world

عجوبہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عجوبه ('ujûbâ), probably from Arabic أُعْجُوبَة (ʔuʕjūba).

عجیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عجیب ('ajīb), from Arabic عَجِيب (ʕajīb). Compare Punjabi ਅਜੀਬ (ajīb) / عَجِیب (ʻajīb).

عدالت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَدَالَت (adālat), from Arabic عَدَالَة (ʕadāla).

عدس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عدس ('adas), from Arabic عَدَس (ʕadas).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

عذاب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَذَاب (azāb), borrowed from Arabic عَذَاب (ʕaḏāb).

senses.g: Iraq (a country in West Asia in the Middle East)

عراق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِراق ('irāq), from Arabic الْعِرَاق (al-ʕirāq).

senses.g: Arabia (a peninsula of West Asia between the Red Sea and the Persian Gulf; includes Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Yemen, Oman, Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates)
Arabia (the region of West Asia within or adjacent to the Arabian Plate; Yemen, Oman, Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, Iraq, Saudi Arabia, Jordan, the Egyptian Sinai, Palestine and the United Arab Emirates)

عربی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَرَبِی (arabī), from Arabic عَرَبِيّ (ʕarabiyy).

عرش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عرش ('arš), from Arabic عَرْش (ʕarš)۔

senses.g: heavenly, celestial, empyrean, ethereal, astral, sublime, divine
one who comes from the highest heaven
the star which is located at highest sky

عرشی.e: From عَرْش ('arś, “sky”) + ـی (-ī). Compare Arabic عَرْشِيّ (ʕaršiyy).

عرضی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عرضی ('arzī), ultimately from Arabic عَرْض (ʕarḍ).

عرق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عرق (araq), from Arabic عَرَق (ʕaraq).
Borrowed from Classical Persian عرق (irq), from Arabic عِرْق (ʕirq).

عروج.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عُرُوج (urūj), from Arabic عُرُوج (ʕurūj). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi عروج ('rvj /⁠'urūj⁠/).

senses.g: a female given name, Urooj or Arooj, from Arabic, meaning “ascension, rise, success”

عروج.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عُرُوج (urūj), from Arabic عُرُوج (ʕurūj). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi عروج ('rvj /⁠'urūj⁠/).

عزت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِزَّت (izzat), borrowed from Arabic عِزَّة (ʕizza), from عَزَّ (ʕazza).

ex.e: respectable household / honorable lineage or house

عزم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَزْم (azm), borrowed from Arabic عَزْم (ʕazm), from عَزَمَ (ʕazama).

عزیز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عزیز ('azīz), from Arabic عَزِيز (ʕazīz).
Borrowed from Classical Persian عزیز ('azīz), from Arabic عَزِيز (ʕazīz).
Borrowed from Classical Persian عزیز ('azīz), from Arabic عَزِيز (ʕazīz).

عسل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَسَل (asal), from Arabic عَسَل (ʕasal).

ex.u: تیرے عشق میں ہم نے کیا کیا نہ دیکھا؟
ex.r: tere iśq mẽ ham ne kyā kyā na dekhā?
ex.e: What did I not see in your love?

عشق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِشْق (išq), borrowed from Arabic عِشْق (ʕišq). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi عشق ('śq /⁠'iśq⁠/).

عطر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عطر ('itr), from Arabic عِطْر (ʕiṭr).

عظم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عظم ('azm), from Arabic عَظْم (ʕaẓm).

ex.e: The spiritual grandeur of the Pakistani Army

عظمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَظَمَت (azamat), from Arabic عَظَمَة (ʕaẓama). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi عظمت ('z̤mt /⁠'azmat⁠/).

عفریت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِفْرِیت (ifrīt), from Arabic عِفْرِيت (ʕifrīt).

عقاب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عُقَاب (uqāb), borrowed from Arabic عُقَاب (ʕuqāb).
Borrowed from Classical Persian عِقَاب (iqāb), borrowed from Arabic عِقَاب (ʕiqāb), from عَاقَبَ (ʕāqaba).

عقل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَقْل ('aql), from Arabic عَقْل (ʕaql). First attested as Old Hindi अकलि (akali).

عقلمند.e: From عَقْل ('aql, “intelligence”) + مَنْد (mand). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi عقلمند ('qlmnd).

senses.g: a female given name, Aqeela or Aqila, from Arabic

عکس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَکْس (aks), borrowed from Arabic عَكْس (ʕaks), from عَكَسَ (ʕakasa).

علاج.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian علاج ('ilāj), from Arabic عِلَاج (ʕilāj).

علاقہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَلَاقَه (alāqa), borrowed from Arabic عَلَاقَة (ʕalāqa). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi علاقہ ('laqh).

علامت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَلَامَت (alāmat), borrowed from Arabic عَلَامَة (ʕalāma). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi علامت ('lamt /⁠'alāmat⁠/).

علف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَلَف (alaf), from Arabic عَلَف (ʕalaf).

علما.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عُلَمَاء (ulamā'), borrowed from Arabic عُلَمَاء (ʕulamāʔ), from عَالِم (ʕālim), from عَلِمَ (ʕalima).

علمی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِلْمِی (ilmī), borrowed from Arabic عِلْمِيّ (ʕilmiyy), from عِلْم (ʕilm) or ـِيّ (-iyy). By surface analysis, علم (ilm) + ـی (-ī).

علوم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عُلُوم (ulūm), borrowed from Arabic عُلُوم (ʕulūm), from عِلْم (ʕilm), from عَلِمَ (ʕalima).

senses.g: a male given name, Ali, from Arabic

علی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَلِی (alī), from Arabic عَلِيّ (ʕaliyy).

senses.g: upon, over, on (only used in borrowed Arabic phrases; see related terms below)

علی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَلَیٰ (alā), from Arabic عَلَى (ʕalā).

senses.g: Aliabad (a city in Hunza district, Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan)
a male given name from Chagatai

علی قلی.e: From Chagatai علیقلی (ʿly-qly, literally “slave of [Imam] Ali”); compare Azerbaijani Əliqulu, Turkish Alikulu.

علیحدگی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian علیحدگی ('alā-hidagī), equivalent to عَلَیحِدَہ ('alaihida) + ـگی (-gī).

senses.g: separate, apart from, asunder, disconnected, distinct

علیحدہ.e: Likely borrowed from Classical Persian علیحده. Univerbation of Arabic عَلَى حِدَة (ʕalā ḥida, “separately, by oneself, by one’s own, being solitary”, literally “on solitariness, upon solitude”). Cognate with Bengali আলাদা (alada).

Likely borrowed from Classical Persian علیحده. Univerbation of Arabic عَلَى حِدَة (ʕalā ḥida, “separately, by oneself, by one’s own, being solitary”, literally “on solitariness, upon solitude”). Cognate with Bengali আলাদা (alada).

senses.g: a female given name, Aleena or Alina, from Arabic

علینہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَلِینَه (alīna), from Arabic عَلِينَة (ʕalīna).

علیہ السلام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَلَیْهِ السَّلَام (alayh-i s-salām), from Arabic عَلَيْهِ السَّلَامُ (ʕalayhi s-salāmu).

senses.g: blessings and peace be upon him

علیہ الصلاۃ والسلام.e: Borrowed from Arabic عَلَيْهِ ٱلصَّلَاةُ وَٱلسَّلَامُ (ʕalayhi ṣ-ṣalātu was-salāmu).

علیہا السلام.e: Borrowed from Arabic عَلَيْهَا السَّلَامُ (ʕalayhā s-salāmu).

عمارت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عمارت ('imārat), from Arabic عِمَارَة (ʕimāra).

senses.g: Oman (a country in West Asia in the Middle East)

عمدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عُمْدَه (umda), from Arabic عُمْدَة (ʕumda).

senses.g: age (number of years one has lived)

عمر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عمر ('umr), taken from Arabic عُمْر (ʕumr).

senses.g: a male given name, Umar or Omar
ʿUmar ibn al-Ḵaṭṭāb, recognized as the Islamic prophet's second rightly-guided successor in Sunni Islam

عمر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عمر ('umr), taken from Arabic عُمْر (ʕumr).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic, equivalent to English Imran
Umerkot (a city in Umerkot district, Sindh, Pakistan)

عمرکوٹ.e: Renaming of امرکوٹ (“Amarkot”) or named after Umar Marvi + کوٹ (koṭ).

senses.g: umrah (a minor hajj, a lesser pilgrimage to Mecca, other than at the time of the hajj)

عمل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عمل ('amal), from Arabic عَمَل (ʕamal).

عموما.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عموما ('umūman), from Arabic عُمُومًا (ʕumūman, “generally”).

عنایت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِنایَت (ināyat), borrowed from Arabic عِنَايَة (ʕināya), from عَنَى (ʕanā). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi عنایت ('nayt /⁠'ināyat⁠/).

senses.g: a unisex given name from Arabic

عنایت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِنایَت (ināyat), borrowed from Arabic عِنَايَة (ʕināya), from عَنَى (ʕanā). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi عنایت ('nayt /⁠'ināyat⁠/).

عنصر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عُنْصُر ('unsur), from Arabic عُنْصُر (ʕunṣur).

عنوان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عُنْوَان (unwān), itself from Arabic عُنْوَان (ʕunwān).

عوام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عوام ('awām), from Arabic عَوَامّ (ʕawāmm), the plural of عَامَّة (ʕāmma). Compare Punjabi ਅਵਾਮ (avām).

عوام الناس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَوَامُّالنَّاس (awāmmu-n-nās), from Arabic عَوَامّ النَّاس (ʕawāmm an-nās).

ex.u: یہ ہم گنہ گار عورتیں ہیں جو اہل جبہ کی تمکنت سے نہ رعب کھائیں نہ جان بیچیں نہ سر جھکائیں نہ ہاتھ جوڑیں
ex.r: ye ham gunahgār aurtẽ ha͠i jo ahl-e-jubbā kī tamaknat se na rob khāẽ na jān becẽ na sar jhukāẽ na hāth joṛẽ
ex.e: It is we sinful women who are not awed by the grandeur of those who wear gowns, who don't sell our lives, who don't bow our heads, who don't fold our hands together

عورت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَوْرَت (awrat), from Arabic عَوْرَة (ʕawra, “genitals; nudity”), where the shift to "woman" in early New Indo-Aryan happened because it was considered immodest to expose the female body in public. First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi عَورَت ('vrt)/औरत (aurata).

عہد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عهد ('ahd), itself borrowed from Arabic عَهْد (ʕahd).

senses.g: enquiry or visit of well-being (of a patient or sick person)

ex.u: آخر ذرا کھڑے کھڑے ایک صاحب کی عیادت کے لیے چلے گئے
ex.e: In the end, they made their way to visit a [sick] gentlemen impromptu

عیادت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عیادت ('iyādat, “visitation of the sick”), itself from Arabic عِيَادَة (ʕiyāda, “visit, consultation”) - verbal noun of عَادَ (ʕāda, “to visit a patient”).

عیار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عیار (iyār), from Arabic عِيَار (ʕiyār).

senses.g: a trickster, a sly, cunning or crafty person
